file_name: Zounds Hearing, Inc. - MANUFACTURING DESIGN MARKETING AGREEMENT.txt

perturbation.original_text: Subcontractor will be also solely responsible for remaining in good standings at all times with all regulatory authorities including but not limited to the standards set forth by the FDA for medical device manufacturing facilities. The Manufacturer may list the address of the Subcontractor's current FDA registered manufacturer's facility as the Manufacturer's own FDA approved manufacturer's facility for the Manufacturer's Products.
perturbation.changed_text: The Manufacturer may list the address of the Subcontractor's current FDA registered manufacturer's facility as the Manufacturer's own FDA approved manufacturer's facility for the Manufacturer's Products.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause that states the 'Subcontractor will be also solely responsible for remaining in good standings at all times with all regulatory authorities including but not limited to the standards set forth by the FDA for medical device manufacturing facilities' creates a potential legal issue. FDA regulations require manufacturers to adhere to specific standards, and removing this responsibility clause could be interpreted as an attempt to circumvent those regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR § 820.20 mandates that manufacturers establish and maintain a quality system that is appropriate for the specific medical device being manufactured. Removing the Subcontractor's responsibility for maintaining good standing with regulatory authorities directly contradicts the requirement for a robust quality system, creating non-compliance.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Quality System (QS) Regulation/Medical Device Current Good Manufacturing Practices (CGMP) | FDA Skip to main content Skip to FDA Search Skip to in this section menu Skip to footer links In this section : Postmarket Requirements (Devices) Home Medical Devices Device Advice: Comprehensive Regulatory Assistance Postmarket Requirements (Devices) Quality System (QS) Regulation/Medical Device Current Good Manufacturing Practices (CGMP) Postmarket Requirements (Devices) Update: January 31, 2024 The FDA issued the Quality Management System Regulation (QMSR) Final Rule to amend the device current good manufacturing practice (CGMP) requirements of the Quality System (QS) regulation (21 CFR Part 820), incorporating by reference the international standard specific for medical device quality management systems set by the International Organization for Standardization (ISO), ISO 13485:2016 Medical devices – Quality management systems – Requirements for regulatory purposes. This final rule is the latest action taken by the FDA to promote consistency in the regulation of devices. This action will harmonize the FDA’s CGMP regulatory framework with that used by other regulatory authorities. The rule is effective February 2, 2026, two years after publication. Until then, manufacturers are required to comply with the QS regulation. Introduction Flexibility of the QS Regulation Applicability of the QS Regulation GMP Exemptions Additional Quality System Information Quality System Regulation and Preamble Introduction Manufacturers must establish and follow quality systems to help ensure that their products consistently meet applicable requirements and specifications. The quality systems for FDA-regulated products (food, drugs, biologics, and devices) are known as current good manufacturing practices (CGMP’s). CGMP requirements for devices in part 820 (21 CFR part 820) were first authorized by section 520(f) of the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (the Act). Under section 520(f) of the Act, FDA issued a final rule in the Federal Register of July 21, 1978 (43 FR 31 508), prescribing CGMP requirements for medical devices. This regulation became effective on December 18, 1978, and was codified under part 820. In 1990, the FDA undertook the start of the revision of the CGMP regulation to add the design controls authorized by the Safe Medical Devices Act. Also, the agency believed that it would be beneficial to the public and the medical device industry for the CGMP regulation to be consistent, to the extent possible, with the requirements for quality systems contained in applicable international standards, primarily, the International Organization for Standard
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the Subcontractor's explicit responsibility for maintaining good standing with regulatory authorities, including FDA standards, which creates a legal contradiction. The original text makes the subcontractor solely responsible. The change removes that language entirely. According to the Law Explanation, this contradicts 21 CFR § 820.20, which mandates manufacturers to maintain a quality system appropriate for the medical device being manufactured. Scraped Law Snippet 2 discusses the Quality System (QS) Regulation/Medical Device Current Good Manufacturing Practices (CGMP). Removing the subcontractor's responsibility undermines the requirement for a robust quality system, potentially leading to non-compliance. The FDA requires manufacturers to meet applicable requirements and specifications which removing responsibility undermines.

perturbation.original_text: Since Manufacturer is paying the actual manufacturing cost of the product, Supplier has not reserved for any warranty related costs. The Manufacturer acknowledges that it is solely responsible for any warranty costs associated with Manufacturer's Products.
perturbation.changed_text: Since Manufacturer is paying the actual manufacturing cost of the product, Supplier has not reserved for any warranty related costs.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the sentence 'The Manufacturer acknowledges that it is solely responsible for any warranty costs associated with Manufacturer's Products,' the contract becomes vague about who bears the warranty costs. This omission could potentially violate consumer protection laws that imply warranties, especially if the products are defective. The manufacturer would have no explicit acknowledgement of warranty responsibility.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Arizona Revised Statutes (ARS) Title 44, Chapter 2, Article 2, sections pertaining to implied warranties of merchantability and fitness for a particular purpose
perturbation.law_explanation: Arizona law A.R.S. § 44-1265 implies a warranty of merchantability in contracts for the sale of goods. By removing the explicit acknowledgement that the Manufacturer is responsible for warranty costs, the contract opens the door to potential violations of this implied warranty, since the responsibilities are not clearly defined. This places a legal risk onto the consumer regarding the product's expected functionality and quality.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 44-1265 - Nonlimitation of rights; refund or replacement not required if certain procedures not followed; attorney fees 44-1265 . Nonlimitation of rights; refund or replacement not required if certain procedures not followed; attorney fees A. If a manufacturer has established or participates in an informal dispute settlement procedure which complies in all respects with 16 Code of Federal Regulations part 703, section 44-1263 relating to refunds or replacement does not apply to any consumer who has not first resorted to such a procedure. B. A consumer shall begin an action under this article within six months following the earlier of expiration of the express warranty term or two years or twenty-four thousand miles following the date of original delivery of the motor vehicle to the consumer, whichever is earlier. If a consumer prevails in an action under this article, the court shall award the consumer reasonable costs and attorney fees.

perturbation.justification: The omission of the manufacturer's acknowledgement of warranty responsibility creates a legal contradiction. The Law Explanation clarifies that Arizona law implies a warranty of merchantability (A.R.S. § 44-1265). By removing the explicit acknowledgement, the changed text weakens the consumer's protection under this implied warranty. While neither snippet explicitly states the implied warranty itself, the Law Explanation, combined with the omission of the statement taking on the responsibility for warranty costs by the Manufacturer, creates a situation where implied warranties under law may not be honored. This undermines the legal obligation to provide products that meet basic standards of merchantability, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: In the event Manufacturer in good faith believes that Manufacturer's Products are nonconforming under the specifications agreed to among the Parties (the "Standards"), Manufacturer shall give written notice to Subcontractor specifying in detail the nonconformity within thirty (30) days of Manufacturer's receipt of such Manufacturer's Products.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event Manufacturer believes that Manufacturer's Products are nonconforming under the specifications agreed to among the Parties (the "Standards"), Manufacturer shall give written notice to Subcontractor specifying in detail the nonconformity within thirty (30) days of Manufacturer's receipt of such Manufacturer's Products.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'in good faith' from the condition for reporting non-conforming goods removes a key protection for the subcontractor against frivolous or malicious claims from the manufacturer. This increases the risk to the subcontractor as it might now have to address claims regardless of their legitimacy. This could cause issues related to quality control standards.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 1-304 imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance and enforcement of every contract. Removing the 'in good faith' requirement for reporting non-conforming goods undermines this duty, potentially allowing the Manufacturer to act opportunistically. This omission creates a legal vulnerability because it deviates from the standard commercial practice that requires honesty and reasonable standards in contractual dealings.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. perturbation.justification: The modification removes the 'in good faith' clause, which directly contradicts UCC § 1-304 as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which says: 'Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' The changed text weakens this legal requirement by allowing the manufacturer to potentially make claims of nonconformity without needing to act in good faith, creating a legal vulnerability as described in the Law Explanation.
